speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the German, they already in. I can't remember on the exactly date or the months. But in the beginning of the year, they already had been in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened-- well, happened is that they took every thing away from the Jew. And we were allowed to go out to shop. We still lived in our own place, everybody. But the situation came worse and worse and worse. We had a few neighbors, very nice neighbors where my parents lived. They brought us a lot of things, then. So we are not starving. And we had everything. And when it became worse, the German came. And they knew everybody's name, every-- every family, how many families they have, what they have, what they don't have. They have it all written down. And they had to give up everything that they had in money, in jewelry, in everything. And it became worse. And then, I think it was end of '44 when the alderman came and said to the people he had an order from the Germans that we have to go into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the end of '44 or--

speaker: interviewee
text: End '44. I mean, middle of '44, I should say. Middle of '44, before they took us away-- middle of '44. You know what the ghetto is?

speaker: interviewer
text: You tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, the ghetto is where the very religious people were living. There is a suburb. It called Sighet. The people were living there. And all the people-- there were about 5,000 Jew in Gyor. They went to that ghetto. We were housed there. There are people living in the flats, in the house. Everywhere you can, you lived. We were lucky, because I had an auntie who lived in that suburb. So we lived with her, all of us, in a two bedroom flat by about 16.

speaker: interviewee
text: 16 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: 16 people in a two bedroom flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two bedroom flat. We were very lucky, because it was her flat. And we had enough to eat. And we were there for quite a few weeks until the German came with the trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for a little bit more about the ghetto, how did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought us in food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought us in food-- not the Germans, the Hungarian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, friends of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends. Friends of ours. They brought us in. I had a picture. But I don't know where it is where I was already pregnant. I was sitting on the balcony when we were already in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your husband was already back from the the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He wasn't. He wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went up. They took him to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they take him--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they take him to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we-- they took him up when we started to go to the ghetto. Then they took all the mens out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were separated from your husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --almost as soon as you would have fallen pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't know. He didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so who did you go to the ghetto with?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she lives there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: With all my family, yes, with my sisters, mother, father. And another friend came. We are 16 of us living there altogether in her place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your feelings at the time? You knew that you were going to have a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Be honest with you, I was young. And when you're young, you don't think. That is because, if I would have thought of it, what would become and how things are, I think I would have never been fallen pregnant or was just a coincident. But as how it's happened, it's happened. I was very upset. My mother was very upset. My father was very upset, too. But we couldn't do anything, because I was already in that time. You couldn't take it away, either. So I just had to go and do what-- whatever become.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shocking. Listen to the news. Listen maybe [NON-ENGLISH] will come and do something. But nobody came. Only the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people were living in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should say about 3,500 thousand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: A Judenrat-- a Jewish council.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish council was there who organized if somebody was sick or we needed a doctor or a child was born or a funeral was on. That's what they did. They couldn't do anything else. I say maybe they were in contact with the outside world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure if we were two months. Approximately, I think we were about two months to three months. I couldn't remember, be honest with you. Approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from the ghetto, where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the ghetto, we were transported in trucks to the train station. And there were waiting the trains in a long, long row, where you actually-- the animals, you putting in there. And we were put in-- in those trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: About in one of the-- how you call it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Cubic. About 50 people to 60. So we were like putting together like ants. There was no toilet, no water, nothing, only the floor. Was there a window?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was one little window. And that was closed, too, and never was open. And from there, we went for hours, days and days in that. There was people dying, because they couldn't get any air. There was children dying, because they couldn't get anything to eat. How myself is pregnant and my parents went through, I don't know until these days. And we arrived. We didn't know where. They just said to us, we're going on a holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea. My father knew it, because he was crying. But he didn't say anything. Soon as we arrived, they opened up the door. They throw out the dead ones. They called us by names. We all went down, like a family. And we walked. And the Germans beside us, with the dogs. When we arrived, we saw written out-- Auschwitz. So when we had arrived, there were lines. And there were Germans. And there were German soldiers, womens. There were Polish girls who were before us there who said to us, aren't you lucky you just arrived. Now, I am here already for a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's they did, they separated us, first of all, from my parents, we three children. My father was separated from my mother. We [INAUDIBLE] we're separated from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we marched. When we marched, they called them the women and the mens [GERMAN]. [GERMAN], that means that they look after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said to me, you see that smoke was coming from there? Said, they cooking? In German, because my language-- my mother language was German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Said, no. They're burning the people there. Said, if your mother and father is old, they going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we marched. And my father was waving for me. And my mother was waving for me. This is the last time I saw my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we marched in. And we came in to that chamber. One side was a gas chamber. The other side was a chamber where all the people were separated from everybody-- children from their mother, mothers from their children, young and old. Everybody was separated. We had to take our clothes off, everything. We went in to another room. I should say the room was-- I can't remember. But it's huge with all the water running from the top. And it was cold, freezing cold in the winter. And they showered us with cold water. When they showered us, they put us in [INAUDIBLE] room. It was cold. And everybody got just one frock. But we put ourself in shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we marched in the snow for a half an hour before we have reached-- how should I explain it to you? Barracks. Like the soldiers what they have, the barracks. And they were A, B, C or 1, 2, 3-- different names, different numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in one of those barracks, there were about 2,000 people. We slept on the floor. We couldn't get any food. The food that they gave us, there was something in the food for the women. They couldn't get the menstrual cycle. They couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Menstruation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was something in the food. The food you couldn't eat. But you ate it, because you were hungry and were dying for hunger and thirst. So we were lying there for months. Until one morning, I couldn't tell you the time. It was 3 o'clock in the morning or 2 o'clock in the morning. The German came with the dogs, out, out, out, out. In the snow. We stood [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means that [INAUDIBLE] was coming. And all the women who was there, we had to stand 1, 2, 3, 4, in a row. There was thousands of them, us. He came to inspect-- actually, we were waiting there one or two hours in that cold until he came for an inspection, who he can take to the gas chamber and who he can take to be working and put us on the train. Because they needed people to work for them. Because everything was [? bombarded, ?] and they didn't have any people. And actually, for us, [? it didn't ?] paid for the people to be paid. But we didn't get any money-- only to work. And I was, then, already four and a half months pregnant-- or three and a half. I can't remember. And as how we were standing [GERMAN]-- my middle sister, she was the first one. I was the second one. My smallest sister third one, and one of our girlfriends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when my middle sister, [? Mengele ?] inspected-- [? show ?] [INAUDIBLE] you are healthy and everything-- he told her to go one the other side. When she went on the other side, and [? Mengele ?] went away from us-- because first row and then he went further-- I told my sister, would you come back and I go there? And that's how I saved my life. He couldn't see me that I was pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my other sisters came to same place like me. They were all healthy to go to work. We went back to our barracks. The next morning we were shifted on the train to work. How long had you been in Auschwitz for by this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz? I think we were there two or three months. I can't remember, really, how long I was there. Do you remember-- It was too long, anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And do you remember when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can't remember the date when we came from Hungary. I don't know if I gave it to you. I can't remember on the right date. Anyhow, when we went on the train and the train was moving, one of Polish girl who were there for a long time already and she notice that I am pregnant. She wanted to pull me out of the train. And then I told her, I have something in my mouth. If she doesn't tell anybody or doesn't pull me off, I give it to her, but only when the train were moving and I went. And that was very naive, because she could have stopped the train or anything to do. So I had a diamond in my mouth. And I gave it to her. I had it in the back in my mouth. And I gave it to her. And that how I saved the second time from Auschwitz. We went to the Lippstadt. That Lippstadt is on the border of Holland and Germany. We worked in a munitions factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were all three [INAUDIBLE] together. And all my pregnancy, I was working day and night, most likely at nighttime. We went for shift work at night time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- describe to me the life in the munitions factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which life?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the munitions factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the munitions camp? Well, the munition camp, when we arrived-- of course, after Auschwitz, it was luxury. We had a wooden bed that we can sleep on it. We had a bathroom that we went to the toilet and wash ourself. And we got some food. We got some food from outside every day. And there was always somebody else who was in the kitchen, who served. Most likely, the people who could speak German-- they took the German people from against us. And when I was with my pregnancy, really on the end, I still went to work. Only the last three weeks of my pregnancy when I was already nine months old, they kept me in the-- at home. But I didn't-- I had to do cleaning. I had to go to the kitchen, different things. When my child was born, it was a very quick birth. She was beautiful. But after that, I became very ill. I couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hide your pregnancy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have to hide it. There, I didn't have to hide it. There was other two woman who was pregnant, too, from different sections. They came from different lagers. Their child was born there, too. And one of them live in America, that child. It's a boy. I can't remember their names, because it was so long ago. And there was a nurse, a Czechoslovakian nurse. There were quite a few Czechoslovakian who where under the Germans. They were politician-- I mean, they brought them. They were also under the Germans like we were, but more freedoms they had. And they could go out and did everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that nurse, she gave me for my-- when I couldn't walk, she took some blood from my arm and put it in my legs. And they warmed up water and everything, said I should [INAUDIBLE]. So it was about two months before I could walk. So after you'd had your baby--

speaker: interviewee
text: After I had my baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you weren't able to walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't. So after two months, I could walk again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you look after your baby in those first--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters. And all, everybody there-- everybody. I mean, we were enough of them. Everybody. And when I could walk, I had-- I didn't have to go back to the factory to work there. I worked in the kitchen and cleaned. So I could provide the baby with everything. The baby was about four months old, five months old when they came. My sister, she worked in the office. The other sister worked in the factory. And she knew all about what's going on, what's coming. Because see heard everything. She worked in the office by the Germans. And one day, she came home and her eyes were cried out. And my other sister's eyes were cried out. And she said, oh, it's nothing, nothing. Somebody's very ill. That's why. But she knew where I have will be-- they will take me away from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have been taken away to Bergen-Belsen with my child, with another two women who had two children, and the sick ones to Bergen-Belsen from Lippstadt. And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That could have been end of '44, nearly end of '44, because the German came. And they were very, very-- how should I say? The English and the American took them already on the throat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were always-- it was the end of the war, already, when they really bombard them very well. And they knew it was the end of the war for them, too. So they took us to Bergen-Belsen. So were they bombarding the munitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. They did. And not only that. The train where we were going-- there were a whole long train, not only from Bergen-Belsen. Everywhere where the concentration camps was there, they took all the old ones and everybody to Bergen-Belsen. And then we went with the train. They bombard the train. And only myself with the other women with the children and a few others who where in that section-- we survived. Nobody else. Everybody died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you look after your baby in Lippstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I look after-- I had some milk. And people were very good from outside. They brought us everything in, from Lippstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there medical assistance as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only when we really needed somebody, only when we really needed. Otherwise, there was nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klara.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klara.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what day she was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg you pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what day she was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born on a Saturday. But I couldn't tell you the date. [? I forgot. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about her birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was beautiful. She was a really lovely, beautiful baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your feelings about having a baby with everythi--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my first one. You can imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But with everything that you were going through, you'd already been through Auschwitz. You'd already lost your parents. What were your feelings about what would happen to your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only hope was that we knew the Germans are on the end. That was our only hope, that we get through and make it out alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was our hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that your life was now at risk because you had a child--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because there, it was quite a freedom. You didn't feel like that you-- we were under the Germans, very much so. We couldn't go out anywhere and do nothing. But still, you it was not in Auschwitz. It was different. It was shocking. But it was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never-- you don't think of things like that, you know? It's unbelievable. And I was young. And you just hoped. And if you don't have any hope, there's no life. And where were your sisters when you left to go to Bergen-Belsen? Where were your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: They still were in Lippstadt at the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister try and help you stay in Lippstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was no hope. We couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you going to? What did you think Bergen-Belsen was going to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had no idea. Another camp. But not what I-- what-- when I went there, what I saw there, thought that's the end of us. And it was nearly the end of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, how long did it take to get from Lippstadt to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horrible, horrible things. When we arrived, there was an-- a big, empty house. We arrived there first. Then they took us. There was a Dutch children lager. They took us there first. And I thought, oh, that was quite all right for the child. But they didn't left us there. And they took us in the same situation it was like in Auschwitz-- even worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: People was dying everywhere. It's how you went. You saw people everywhere-- young, old, men, women, children. Everywhere. We came to a lager. They said it's a hospital. It wasn't a hospital. It was just a lager. And there were beds-- four beds after each other. I got the bed on the bottom with my child. And it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had enough meal to feed her for a while. And not far from that lager, there was a Croats. And one night, I didn't have enough to feed her. So I went out. They wanted to exchange something for food. And the Croats saw me. And they hit me, but so much that I even couldn't crawl back to the child, to my lager. Because I was unconscious until the next morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lied there in the snow until next morning. I woke up. And I crawled back. By the next morning I crawled back, she was already dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of the Germans came, said, put her in your arms. Kiss her goodbye. And throw her out. So they took me not far from there where I was. And there was mountains and mountains of dead people. So I threw her out there. And he took me back. We'll just cut there.
